signals: The most effective treatment for adhesive capsulitis is uncertain
Nonsurgical treatments include nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, short-term oral corticosteroids
Surgical treatment options for patients who have minimal improvement after six to 12 weeks
can persist for years with some patients never regaining full function
Physiotherapy and corticosteroid injections combined may provide greater improvement

extraction.summary: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) is a common shoulder condition characterized by pain and decreased range of motion, especially in external rotation, with increased prevalence in patients with diabetes mellitus and hypothyroidism. Recent evidence suggests persistent functional limitations if left untreated, challenging the traditional theory of complete self-resolution within 1-2 years.

population.risk_criteria: Female gender, diabetes mellitus (5x increased risk), hypothyroidism (27.2% vs 10.7% prevalence)
population.disease_definition: Pathologic process with contracture of the glenohumeral capsule presenting as pain, stiffness, and dysfunction of the affected shoulder
population.exclusions: Fever, night sweats, malaise, unexplained weight loss (suggesting neoplasm or autoimmune disease), neuropathic symptoms (suggesting cervical radiculopathy)

key_thresholds.parameter: Diabetes prevalence in adhesive capsulitis patients

recommendations.recommendation: Consider testing persons with adhesive capsulitis for diabetes mellitus or hypothyroidism
recommendations.notes: Based on increased association with these conditions

recommendations.recommendation: The combination of physiotherapy and corticosteroid injection may provide greater symptom improvement than physiotherapy alone
recommendations.notes: May provide greater improvement than physiotherapy alone

recommendations.recommendation: The combination of hydrodilatation and corticosteroid injection may expedite recovery of pain free range of motion compared with corticosteroid injection alone
recommendations.notes: Greatest benefit experienced within first three months

medications.indication: Short-term use only (less than 6 weeks) for pain relief

red_flags: Fever, night sweats, malaise, or unexplained weight loss
Dyspnea or cough (if Pancoast tumor suspected)
extraction.follow_up: Assessment at 6-12 weeks to determine response to nonsurgical treatment
extraction.escalation_path: Referral to orthopedic surgeon for patients with minimal improvement after 6-12 weeks of nonsurgical treatment and who cannot tolerate symptoms. Surgical options include manipulation under anesthesia and arthroscopic capsule release.
